skill.name: free-tool-strategy
skill.description: When the user wants to plan, evaluate, or build a free tool for marketing purposes — lead generation, SEO value, or brand awareness. Also use when the user mentions "engineering as marketing," "free tool," "marketing tool," "calculator," "generator," "interactive tool," "lead gen tool," "build a tool for leads," or "free resource." This skill bridges engineering and marketing — useful for founders and technical marketers.
skill.summary: When the user wants to plan, evaluate, or build a free tool for marketing purposes — lead generation...
skill.author: sickn33

security_audit.summary: Static analysis flagged Markdown code fences, ordinary marketing text, and tracking terminology as risky patterns. Manual review found no executable code, no cryptographic implementation, no reconnaissance behavior, and no prompt injection attempt. The skill is low risk and safe to publish.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: Markdown Code Fence
low_findings.description: The Ruby or shell backtick finding is a Markdown fence around an ROI projection template, not executable code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line starts a fenced text block and the surrounding lines contain business calculation placeholders. No command interpreter or shell invocation is present.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: Closing Markdown Fence
low_findings.description: The backtick finding is the closing fence for a plain ROI template, not command execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line only closes a Markdown block. It cannot execute commands by itself.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: Output Template Fence
low_findings.description: The backtick finding opens a Markdown example of the expected strategy document format, not Ruby or shell execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding content is a documentation template with headings and placeholders. No executable command or dynamic input handling exists.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: Weak Crypto Keyword
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm finding matched the word "marketing" in the description, not a cryptographic primitive.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is YAML front matter describing a marketing strategy skill. It contains no hashing, encryption, or authentication code.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: Planning Language
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm finding matched normal planning text, not security-sensitive code.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line introduces business context questions. There is no cryptographic API, algorithm name, key material, or password handling.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: Design Terminology
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm finding matched the phrase "Perfect design," which is unrelated to cryptography.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a product scoping recommendation about what to skip initially. It does not reference any cryptographic behavior.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: Lead Capture Wording
low_findings.description: The weak cryptographic algorithm finding matched the word "Capture" in a lead capture template, not credential or secret handling.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is a placeholder for marketing lead capture strategy. No storage, encryption, credential collection, or code path is present.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: System Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The system reconnaissance finding matched UTM tracking guidance for campaign attribution, not host or environment discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line refers to marketing attribution parameters. It does not inspect files, processes, users, network interfaces, or system configuration.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: Network Reconnaissance
low_findings.description: The network reconnaissance finding matched a generic note about inaccessible data, not scanning or network discovery.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line is part of an ideation question about customer information needs. No network target, probing instruction, or data collection mechanism appears.

low_findings.title: Static Finding Dismissed: Sales Process Wording
low_findings.description: The network reconnaissance finding matched product promotion guidance, not network enumeration or scanning.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The line says to mention the tool during sales. It contains no network operation, external request, or reconnaissance instruction.

content.value_statement: Teams often build free tools without a clear link to buyers, search demand, or conversion goals. This skill turns tool ideas into focused plans for lead generation, SEO, and brand awareness.
actual_capabilities: Evaluates free tool ideas against audience fit, search demand, lead quality, and build effort.
Suggests tool formats such as calculators, generators, quizzes, checkers, and comparison tools.
Defines lead capture approaches, including ungated, partially gated, and fully gated flows.
Outlines MVP scope, maintenance needs, success metrics, and promotion channels.
Produces a structured strategy document for deciding whether to build a tool.
limitations: It does not build or deploy the tool by itself.
It needs accurate business, audience, and resource context from the user.
It does not provide live keyword volume or competitor data without external research.
It cannot guarantee leads, rankings, backlinks, or revenue outcomes.

use_cases.title: Prioritize Tool Ideas
use_cases.description: Compare several free tool concepts and select the one with the best balance of demand, buyer fit, and build effort.

use_cases.title: Create a Lead Generation Plan
use_cases.description: Define the tool concept, lead capture method, follow-up path, and success metrics before assigning design or engineering work.

use_cases.title: Scope an MVP
use_cases.description: Turn a promising calculator, quiz, or generator idea into a practical first version with clear launch and maintenance needs.

prompt_templates.title: Brainstorm Free Tool Ideas
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me brainstorm free tool ideas for this business: [business]. Our audience is [audience], and our main product is [product].

prompt_templates.title: Evaluate One Tool Concept
prompt_templates.prompt: Evaluate this free tool idea: [idea]. Score it for audience pain, SEO demand, lead quality, product fit, build effort, and maintenance.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Tool Strategy
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a free tool strategy for [tool idea]. Include target audience, value proposition, lead capture, MVP scope, metrics, and promotion plan.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Build Options
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare these free tool concepts: [list]. Recommend one to build first, explain tradeoffs, and propose a phased rollout plan.

output_examples.input: A B2B payroll startup wants a free tool for HR managers.
output_examples.output: Recommended concept: payroll cost calculator. It has clear buyer intent, natural product fit, and measurable lead capture through emailed reports.

output_examples.input: An agency is considering a website audit checker.
output_examples.output: Best approach: start with a narrow audit for one audience segment. Use partial gating after showing three findings to protect trust.

output_examples.input: A SaaS team has five tool ideas and limited engineering time.
output_examples.output: Prioritize the idea with repeat search demand, low maintenance burden, and a direct path to a product demo request.

best_practices: Start with the buyer problem and product connection before choosing a tool format.
Keep the first version useful, narrow, and easy to maintain.
Measure both acquisition quality and conversion quality after launch.
anti_patterns: Building a clever tool that attracts users who will never buy.
Gating every result before proving that the tool provides value.
Launching without an owner for maintenance, promotion, and follow-up.

faq.question: What kind of tools does this skill help plan?
faq.answer: It helps plan calculators, generators, quizzes, checkers, comparison tools, assessments, and other interactive resources.

faq.question: Can it choose the best idea from several options?
faq.answer: Yes. It can compare ideas using audience pain, search demand, lead quality, product fit, build effort, and maintenance burden.

faq.question: Does it provide implementation code?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on strategy, scope, metrics, and launch planning before implementation begins.

faq.question: Does it support SEO planning?
faq.answer: Yes. It prompts for target keywords, search intent, competition, and link-building potential.

faq.question: How should lead capture be handled?
faq.answer: The skill helps choose ungated, partially gated, or fully gated flows based on trust, value, and conversion goals.

faq.question: Who benefits most from this skill?
faq.answer: Founders, growth marketers, product managers, and technical marketers who use useful tools to attract qualified demand.
